metadata.title: Reinforcement Learning: An Introduction (Sutton & Barto) — Book Review
metadata.color_semantics: claude fidelity: INK #3D3929; SPARK #D97757 one accent.
metadata.author: Nik Bear Brown
metadata.credit_line: Nik Bear Brown — Founder, Humanitarians AI
metadata.note: HAI Fellows batch v2 — Teardown register, @NikBearBrown, Remotion animated bookends. Credit: Nik Bear Brown — Founder, Humanitarians AI. HONESTY: all numbers trace to source article.

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. Value functions v_pi and q_pi capture the long-term consequences of actions, not just immediate reward. Optimistic initialization pushes early exploration but that bias fades as estimates get corrected. Control variates cut variance without shifting the expected value of an estimate. Sarsa, Expected Sarsa, and Tree Backup all generalize into n-step forms of the same idea.

artifactLines: Value functions v_pi and q_pi capture the long-term consequences of actions, not just immediate reward.
Optimistic initialization pushes early exploration but that bias fades as estimates get corrected.
Control variates cut variance without shifting the expected value of an estimate.
Sarsa, Expected Sarsa, and Tree Backup all generalize into n-step forms of the same idea.

beats.narration_text: I want to feel the difference between the tabular methods this covered rather than just memorize their names. Take a simple 5-state random walk and walk me through one update each for Sarsa, Expected Sarsa, and Tree Backup so I can see why Expected Sarsa has lower variance. Then show me how optimistic initialization changes early action selection. Please give me: (1) the three update equations side by side, (2) a worked numeric step for each, (3) one plot idea that would reveal the variance gap.

Reinforcement learning crystallizes around three signals: state,
The k-armed bandit is reinforcement learning stripped to essentia
